items.question_text: Under 8-1-1, when do the procedures in a directive or letter of agreement supersede the default Chapter 8 procedures?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-1, Chapter 8 procedures apply except when a directive or letter of agreement prescribes different procedures or minima.

choices.text: Only when the FAA Administrator personally signs the directive.
Only for VFR operations in offshore airspace areas.
Never — Chapter 8 is the supreme authority for all oceanic operations.
When the directive or LOA prescribes different procedures or minima for the covered situation.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-1, what reference paragraph covers procedural letters of agreement?
items.explanation: Per the reference block of 8-1-1, FAA Order JO 7110.65 paragraph 1-1-10 covers Procedural Letters of Agreement.

choices.text: Paragraph 2-1-29, RVSM Operations.
Paragraph 1-1-10, Procedural Letters of Agreement (LOAs).
Paragraph 5-1-1, Application of Radar.
Paragraph 8-1-2, Operations in Offshore Airspace Areas.

items.question_text: A controller faces heavy oceanic traffic and decides to follow a local facility directive instead of the default Chapter 8 procedure for that flow. Under 8-1-1, is this permissible?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-1, a directive or LOA that prescribes different procedures or minima supersedes the default Chapter 8 rule. The controller may follow the directive if it covers the situation.

choices.text: No, because Chapter 8 always overrides any local-facility directive in oceanic controlled airspace.
Yes, because 8-1-1 permits directives and LOAs to prescribe different procedures or minima that replace the default Chapter 8 rule.
Only if the aircraft is VFR and below FL180.
No, because facility directives are never referenced in Chapter 8.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-2, complete the hierarchy: when a situation is not covered by Chapter 8, what applies?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-2, the provisions in the Order apply for situations not covered by Chapter 8.

choices.text: the provisions in this Order

items.question_text: Under 8-1-2, how does the offshore airspace rule differ from the oceanic controlled airspace rule in 8-1-1?
items.explanation: 8-1-1 for oceanic controlled airspace says directives and LOAs may supersede Chapter 8. 8-1-2 for offshore airspace says Chapter 8 applies, and when Chapter 8 is silent the rest of the Order applies. 8-1-2 does not separately mention directives or LOAs as superseding authorities.

choices.text: 8-1-2 applies only to VFR flights, while 8-1-1 applies only to IFR flights.
They are identical — both list directives and LOAs as superseding authorities.
8-1-2 forbids using any part of the Order outside Chapter 8, unlike 8-1-1.
8-1-2 does not separately list directives or LOAs as authorities that may prescribe different procedures; it only says the Order applies when Chapter 8 is silent.

items.question_text: A controller in offshore airspace needs to handle a wake turbulence situation not addressed in Chapter 8. The trainee says to ignore it because Chapter 8 is the only authority offshore. Is the trainee correct?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-2, for situations not covered by Chapter 8 the provisions in the Order apply, so wake turbulence rules elsewhere in the Order would be used.

choices.text: Yes — offshore airspace always defaults to ICAO procedures when Chapter 8 is silent.
Yes — Chapter 8 is the only authority in offshore airspace and no other part of JO 7110.65 applies.
No — when Chapter 8 does not cover a situation, the relevant provisions elsewhere in the Order apply.
Only if the aircraft reports IFR; VFR operations are exempt.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-3, VFR flight plan operations in Oceanic FIRs are permitted only between _____ and _____. Fill in both blanks.
items.explanation: Per 8-1-3, operations on a VFR flight plan are permitted only between sunrise and sunset.

choices.text: sunrise, sunset

items.question_text: Under 8-1-3, in the Miami, Houston, and San Juan Oceanic CTAs, VFR flight plan operations are limited to what altitude?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-3a, operations in those CTAs are permitted only below FL 180.

choices.text: below FL 180

items.question_text: Under 8-1-3, which is NOT one of the three areas where VFR flight plan operations are permitted in Oceanic FIRs?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-3, the permitted areas are: Miami/Houston/San Juan CTAs below FL180; Oakland FIR less than 100 NM seaward within controlled airspace; all Oceanic FIR airspace below the Oceanic CTAs. The New York Oceanic CTA is not listed.

choices.text: The Miami, Houston, and San Juan Oceanic CTAs below FL180.
The New York Oceanic CTA below FL180.
The Oakland FIR when operating less than 100 NM seaward from the shoreline within controlled airspace.
All Oceanic FIR airspace below the Oceanic CTAs.

items.question_text: A pilot requests a VFR flight plan operation in the Oakland FIR at night, 50 NM seaward, with weather exceeding basic VFR minima. Under 8-1-3, may this operation be conducted?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-3, VFR flight plan operations are permitted only between sunrise and sunset, regardless of weather.

choices.text: Yes, because the flight is within 100 NM seaward and weather is above basic VFR minima.
No, because VFR flight plan operations are permitted only between sunrise and sunset.
Yes, because the Oakland FIR has no nighttime restriction.
Yes, because VFR operations at night only require basic VFR weather minima.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-3, what 14 CFR section specifies the weather minimums applicable to VFR flights in Oceanic FIRs?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-3, VFR flights may be conducted in conditions equal to or greater than those specified in 14 CFR section 91.155, Basic VFR weather minimums.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-4, horizontal separation in oceanic/offshore procedures consists of either _____ or _____ separation. Fill both blanks.
items.explanation: Per 8-1-4b, horizontal separation may be either longitudinal or lateral.

choices.text: longitudinal, lateral

items.question_text: Under 8-1-4, which of the following is NOT listed as an acceptable separation type?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-4, the three types are vertical, horizontal (longitudinal or lateral), and radar where coverage is adequate. Visual separation is not listed.

choices.text: Radar separation where coverage is adequate.
Vertical separation.
Longitudinal separation.
Visual separation.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-4, what is the minimum number of separation methods that must be applied between two aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-4, separation must consist of at least one of the following methods. A single approved method is sufficient.

choices.text: At least two methods must be applied simultaneously.
At least one approved method.
All three types must be used in oceanic airspace.
Vertical and horizontal must both be applied at all times.

items.question_text: A trainee claims that radar separation can be used anywhere in oceanic airspace because Chapter 5 always applies. Is this correct under 8-1-4?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-4c, radar separation is acceptable where radar coverage is adequate. It is not automatically available everywhere.

choices.text: Yes — any controller can use radar separation regardless of coverage if the aircraft are IFR.
Yes — Chapter 5 radar separation applies in all oceanic airspace without restriction.
No — radar separation is only acceptable in offshore airspace, never in oceanic airspace.
No — radar separation is only acceptable where radar coverage is adequate.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-5, within oceanic control areas, unless directed or charted otherwise, altitude assignment must be based on flight levels and a standard altimeter setting of _____ inches Hg.
items.explanation: Per 8-1-5, the standard altimeter setting is 29.92 inches Hg.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-5, what two conditions allow departure from the flight-level/29.92 rule for altitude assignment in oceanic control areas?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-5, the rule applies unless directed and/or charted otherwise.

choices.text: When radar coverage is inadequate or when communications are lost.
When the pilot requests it or when turbulence is reported.
When the aircraft is VFR or when separation is not required.
When directed otherwise or when charted otherwise.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-5, how does the oceanic altimeter setting rule differ from what controllers typically use in domestic airspace?
items.explanation: In domestic airspace below FL180, controllers assign a local altimeter setting and altitudes in feet MSL. Per 8-1-5, oceanic altitude assignments use flight levels and the standard 29.92 setting unless directed/charted otherwise.

choices.text: Oceanic altitude assignments use barometric setting only at the departure airport.
Oceanic altitude assignments are identical to domestic rules — use local altimeter settings below FL180.
Oceanic altitude assignments ignore altimeter settings entirely and use only GPS altitude.
Oceanic altitude assignments are based on flight levels and standard 29.92 rather than local altimeter settings and MSL altitudes.

items.question_text: Which source governs when both a directive and a letter of agreement prescribe different oceanic procedures for the same traffic flow?

choices.text: Only the directive counts — letters of agreement are not mentioned in 8-1-1.
Both are mentioned in 8-1-1 as authorities that may supersede the default Chapter 8 procedures; the controller should follow whichever one applies to the specific operation per the directive or LOA.
Only the LOA counts because 8-1-1 lists LOAs first.
Neither counts — 8-1-1 always requires using Chapter 8 regardless of directives or LOAs.

items.explanation: 8-1-1 Lists 'a directive or a letter of agreement' as authorities that may prescribe different procedures or minima.

items.instruction: Where should you look for the rules on procedural letters of agreement?
items.question_text: You need to understand the requirements for procedural LOAs referenced in 8-1-1. Which JO 7110.65 paragraph contains these procedures?

choices.text: Paragraph 5-1-1, Application of Radar.
Paragraph 2-1-29, RVSM Operations.
Paragraph 8-2-1, Coordination.
Paragraph 1-1-10, Procedural Letters of Agreement (LOAs).

items.explanation: Per the reference block of 8-1-1, paragraph 1-1-10 covers Procedural Letters of Agreement.

items.question_text: When Chapter 8 procedures and minima are applied in offshore airspace areas, what is the relationship between Chapter 8 and the rest of JO 7110.65?

choices.text: Chapter 8 and the rest of the Order are alternatives; the controller chooses whichever is easier.
Chapter 8 is advisory only; the rest of the Order always takes precedence.
Chapter 8 applies first, and for any situation not covered by Chapter 8, the provisions in the rest of the Order apply.
Only Chapter 8 applies; the rest of the Order may never be used in offshore airspace.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-2, Chapter 8 provides the primary procedures and minima, and the rest of the Order fills any gaps.

items.situation: You are handling an offshore airspace operation involving emergency assistance procedures. Chapter 8 does not address emergency procedures for this specific offshore scenario.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-2, provide ATC service in offshore airspace in accordance with Chapter 8 procedures and minima. For situations not covered by this chapter, the provisions in this Order apply.

choices.text: Tell the pilot to contact the nearest coastal facility only, because Chapter 8 does not authorize emergency procedures offshore.
Apply the emergency assistance procedures found elsewhere in JO 7110.65 because Chapter 8 does not cover the situation.
Use ICAO emergency procedures only and disregard JO 7110.65.
Suspend all ATC service to that aircraft because FAA procedures do not apply when Chapter 8 is silent.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-2, when Chapter 8 does not cover a situation, the provisions in the Order apply — including emergency procedures in other chapters.

items.question_text: Which is true about VFR flight plan operations in Oceanic FIRs under 8-1-3?

choices.text: They are permitted at any time in any Oceanic FIR as long as the aircraft reports weather at or above 14 CFR 91.155.
They require weather at or above 14 CFR 91.155 minimums, must be between sunrise and sunset, and are limited to three specified areas.
They are permitted only in the Oakland FIR at any altitude below FL600 if within 200 NM of shore.
They are permitted only below FL180 in all Oceanic FIRs regardless of time or location.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-3, VFR flight-plan operations require basic VFR weather, must be daytime only, and are limited to Miami/Houston/San Juan CTAs below FL180, Oakland FIR less than 100 NM seaward, or all Oceanic FIR airspace below the CTAs.

items.situation: A pilot requests a VFR flight plan from Miami to San Juan at FL190 within the Miami Oceanic CTA. Weather is clear and it is midday.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-3, VFR flight-plan operations in the Miami, Houston, and San Juan Oceanic CTAs are permitted only below FL 180, between sunrise and sunset, and in weather at or above 14 CFR 91.155.

choices.text: Approve the flight plan because the weather and time conditions are met.
Deny the VFR flight plan at FL190 because VFR operations in the Miami Oceanic CTA are limited to below FL180.
Approve the flight plan if the pilot files IFR instead but operates VFR in practice.
Approve the flight plan because FL190 is within the all-Oceanic-FIR exception below the CTAs.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-3a, VFR flight-plan operations in the Miami, Houston, and San Juan Oceanic CTAs are permitted only below FL 180.

items.question_text: A controller uses lateral separation between two aircraft in oceanic airspace. Under 8-1-4, is this acceptable?

choices.text: No, because lateral separation is not listed in 8-1-4 among the approved types.
Yes, because lateral separation is a form of horizontal separation, which is one of the approved separation types.
No, because lateral separation may only be used in offshore airspace, not oceanic airspace.
Only if vertical separation cannot be established, as a last resort.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-4b, horizontal separation includes both longitudinal and lateral. Lateral separation is an approved method.

items.situation: You are working an oceanic sector with no radar coverage. Two aircraft at the same flight level are on crossing tracks. You need to establish separation.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-4, separation must consist of at least one of the following: vertical separation; horizontal separation by longitudinal or lateral means; or radar separation where radar coverage is adequate.

choices.text: Rely on radar separation only because crossing-track aircraft always need Chapter 5 radar rules.
Use vertical separation by assigning one aircraft a different flight level, or use horizontal separation by longitudinal or lateral means.
Tell the pilots to maintain visual separation because oceanic sectors always have visual conditions.
Apply no separation because crossing tracks are automatically separated by time.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-4, without radar coverage, the controller must use vertical or horizontal (longitudinal or lateral) separation.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-5, a controller is working an oceanic control area and an en route chart depicts a different altimeter setting procedure for a portion of the sector. What should the controller do in that charted area?

choices.text: Apply the charted procedure only within 50 NM of the shoreline.
Ignore the chart and use flight levels with 29.92 because 8-1-5 is the controlling authority.
Use whichever procedure the pilot prefers for the area in question.
Follow the charted procedure because 8-1-5 permits departure from the flight-level/29.92 rule when charted otherwise.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-5, the flight-level/29.92 rule applies unless directed and/or charted otherwise. A charted exception takes precedence.

items.situation: An aircraft is transitioning from domestic airspace into an oceanic control area. The pilot is still using the coastal altimeter setting and is flying at an assigned MSL altitude.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-5, within oceanic control areas, unless directed or charted otherwise, altitude assignment must be based on flight levels and a standard altimeter setting of 29.92 inches Hg.

choices.text: Ignore the altimeter setting because oceanic operations do not require a standard barometric reference.
Allow the aircraft to keep the coastal altimeter setting because transitioning is optional once offshore.
Instruct the pilot to use the destination altimeter setting throughout the oceanic portion.
If no direction or charting provides otherwise, transition the aircraft to flight levels with the standard 29.92 altimeter setting upon entering the oceanic control area.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-5, within oceanic control areas, altitude assignments use flight levels and the standard 29.92 setting unless directed or charted otherwise.
